content:
6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

I’ve been writing code professionally for close to eight years now. Python and JavaScript, the usual suspects. So when a friend asked me to help her set up a website for her pottery studio, my first instinct was to fire up VS Code and start scaffolding a React app.
Then I stopped myself. She needed a five-page site with a contact form and a gallery, live by the weekend. Not a codebase. Not a deployment pipeline. Just a website.
That project sent me down a rabbit hole. Over the next several months, I ended up building five different sites using no-code and low-code tools — for the pottery studio, a friend’s consulting business, a local nonprofit, a newsletter I run on the side, and a portfolio site for my cousin who’s a photographer. Here’s what actually happened, warts and all.
Why I Even Tried This
I’ll be honest — there was a part of my ego that resisted. Years of learning to code, and now I’m dragging boxes around a screen?
But the more I thought about it, the more the resistance felt like pride rather than logic. Most small projects don’t need custom code. They need something that works, looks decent, and can be updated by someone who isn’t me six months from now. That last part turned out to be the real lesson of this whole experiment.
Site One: The Pottery Studio
This was my first real test, and I went in with low expectations. I used a builder with a visual drag-and-drop editor, picked a template meant for creative businesses, and swapped out the images and text.
It took about four hours, including the time I spent second-guessing font choices. The site looked clean. My friend could log in and change her class schedule herself, which is the part that actually mattered. I’d built her a custom site before on WordPress, and she never touched it again because she was scared of breaking something. This time, she updates it monthly.
The lesson here: the “best” tool isn’t the one with the most features. It’s the one the actual owner will keep using after I walk away.
Site Two: The Consulting Business
My friend’s consulting site needed something more polished—case studies, a booking calendar, and testimonials. I reached for a different builder this time, one known for more design flexibility.
This is where I hit my first real wall. The flexibility was great until I wanted something slightly outside the template’s assumptions — a two-column layout that collapsed differently on mobile than the builder’s default. I spent almost an hour fighting with nested sections before I found a workaround using the platform’s custom CSS field.
Yes, I broke my own “no code” rule a little. Turns out most of these platforms let you drop in a bit of custom CSS or embed code when you need it, and that’s not cheating — it’s just using the tool properly.
What surprised me
The booking integration took ten minutes. Something that would’ve taken me half a day to build from scratch with an API and a calendar library was just a plugin toggle. That’s the trade-off in a nutshell: you give up some control, you get back enormous amounts of time.
Site Three: The Nonprofit
This one had different constraints. Volunteers needed to edit the content, not just the person who set it up. Multiple people, varying technical comfort, and no budget for training.
I picked a platform with the simplest possible editing interface, even though it meant sacrificing some design polish. It genuinely killed me a little to use rounder corners and less custom typography than I wanted. But three months later, the nonprofit’s volunteer coordinator told me she’d added an entire new events page without asking anyone for help.
That’s a win I wouldn’t have gotten if I’d built something clever in code that only I understood.
Site Four: My Own Newsletter Page
For my own project, I went a little rogue and used a builder that leans more technical — one that lets you inject custom HTML blocks and connect things like Zapier or Make for automations. I wired up a simple flow where new subscribers get tagged based on which lead magnet they downloaded.
This is the site where the no-code approach felt closest to real development. I was thinking in terms of triggers and conditions, just without writing the underlying functions myself. It scratched the same itch as coding, honestly, just with different tools.
A small gripe
Debugging automations built with visual tools is oddly harder than debugging code sometimes. There’s no stack trace to squint at—just a flow that silently didn’t fire, and you’re left clicking through nodes trying to guess why. I learned to test each step individually before connecting the whole chain. Small lesson, saved me a lot of frustration later.
Site Five: The Photography Portfolio
By the fifth site, I’d developed opinions. My cousin needed something visually striking above all else—big images, minimal text, and fast load times. I picked a builder that’s more design-forward, even though its content management felt clunkier than the others.
This taught me the biggest lesson of the whole experiment: there’s no single best tool. Each platform makes different trade-offs between design freedom, ease of editing, and how much technical fiddling you’re willing to do. What worked beautifully for the nonprofit would have felt restrictive for the photographer, and what worked for the photographer would have overwhelmed the nonprofit’s volunteers.
What I’d Tell Any Developer Considering This
Don’t treat it as beneath you. I used to see no-code tools as training wheels. After five projects, I see them as a legitimate part of the toolkit — the same way I’d reach for a framework instead of writing everything from scratch.
Match the tool to the person who’ll maintain it, not to your own preferences. This was the single biggest factor in whether a site stayed useful or got abandoned.
Budget time for the 20% that isn’t drag-and-drop. Every single project had at least one moment where I needed custom code, a workaround, or a support thread to solve something the visual editor couldn’t handle cleanly. That’s normal. Plan for it instead of being surprised by it.
Test with real content early. Templates look great with placeholder text and stock photos. They fall apart fast once you drop in a client’s actual, messier content. I started importing real text and images in the first hour of every project after learning this the hard way on site two.
Your dev skills still matter. Understanding how the web actually works — DNS, hosting, basic CSS, how forms submit — made me faster at every one of these builds than someone starting from zero. No-code doesn’t mean no knowledge.
Would I Do It Again?
Yes, without hesitation. Not for everything — if a client needs a real web app with custom logic and a database, I’m still opening my editor. But for marketing sites, portfolios, small business pages, and anything where the owner needs to make their own updates, these tools do a genuinely good job now.
If you’re weighing which platform actually fits your situation, I found it useful to see the major options laid out side by side rather than guessing—this comparison of the best drag-and-drop website builders covers the differences in more depth than I have room for here.
Five sites in, my biggest takeaway isn’t about any particular tool. It’s that the right question was never "Can I build this without code?" but "Who's going to maintain this, and what do they actually need?” Once I started asking that first, the rest got a lot easier.
